ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Respiratory Assessment for Nurses (Inspection, Palpation, Percussion, Auscultation)
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Respiratory Assessment for Nurses | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Clinical review: Respiratory Assessment for Nurses — Respiratory. Board-style clinical judgment: priorities, red flags, and first-line nursing actions.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Respiratory Assessment for Nurses (Inspection, Palpation, Percussion, Auscultation)** (Respiratory) links assessment, oxygen delivery, airway management, infection prevention, and critical care monitoring to nursing judgment: protect **airway and breathing**, titrate **oxygen per orders and targets**, recognize **acute deterioration** (silent chest, fatigue, rising CO₂, tension pneumothorax), support **ventilation and chest drainage** safely, and escalate when respiratory failure threatens perfusion or mentation.

Canadian items may use **metric** units and provincial isolation wording; prioritization logic matches NCLEX-RN.

**Pathway context (RN, Canada).** This lesson supports NCLEX-RN preparation with Canada-friendly practice framing (SI measures where shown, interprofessional norms). Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **inspection, work of breathing, SpO₂, ABG when shown, breath sounds, ventilator/chest tube cues, and mentation** to identify respiratory emergencies and complications.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, RT and provider plans, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **complete obstruction**, **tension pneumothorax**, **massive hemothorax**, **ventilator failure**, **sudden desaturation with altered LOC**, or **rapid clinical decline**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Respiratory Assessment for Nurses (Inspection, Palpation, Percussion, Auscultation)** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Items cluster **inspection** (accessory muscle use, tripod, cyanosis), **palpation** (tracheal deviation, crepitus), **percussion** (dull vs hyperresonant), and **auscultation** (air entry, adventitious sounds)—prioritize **objective findings** that signal **airway compromise**, **pneumothorax**, or **lobar collapse** over reassurance alone.

Eliminate answers that **skip airway assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show instability, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Inspection**: RR pattern, nasal flaring, retractions, speaking in full sentences vs one-word answers, skin colour, anxiety level.
- **Palpation**: chest expansion symmetry; **tracheal deviation** with tension themes; **subcutaneous emphysema** after trauma or line placement.
- **Percussion**: **dullness** suggests fluid or consolidation; **hyperresonance** suggests air trapping or pneumothorax when paired with exam findings.
- **Auscultation**: compare sides; note **absent sounds**, **crackles**, **wheezes**, **pleural rub**; correlate with SpO₂ and work of breathing.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.
- **Oxygen toxicity / hyperoxia in retainers**: somnolence with **rising PaCO₂**—titrate to **ordered SpO₂ targets** and escalate ventilation support when acidosis worsens.
- **Tension pneumothorax**: hypotension, **distended neck veins**, unilateral absent sounds, tracheal shift (late) → emergency decompression themes; do not delay for routine imaging when stem portrays classic shock.
- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.
- **Ventilator alarms**: **never silence without assessment**; treat **client** before **machine** unless circuit disconnect is obvious and safe to fix immediately.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **device use** (NC, mask, CPAP), **signs of worsening** (worsening dyspnea, new cyanosis, fever), **infection prevention** at home when applicable, and **when to call emergency services**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client scenario related to **increasing dyspnea after surgery with diminished breath sounds at one base** brings respiratory risks to the foreground. You pair **focused assessment** (work of breathing, auscultation, SpO₂ trend, mental status, device and tube checks) with **vitals and labs** when provided, maintain **oxygen and isolation per orders**, follow **ventilation and drainage plans**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** You compare competing tasks. The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually **assess airway**, **support breathing**, **verify tube/circuit**, **relieve life threats**, or **obtain critical diagnostics**—rather than routine comfort measures alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **stridor with drooling**, **sudden unilateral absent breath sounds with shock**, **high-pressure vent alarm with desaturation**, or **massive hemothorax**, prioritize **life-saving sequences** that match unit protocol and NCLEX-RN expectations.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **RR + work of breathing + SpO₂ + mentation + breath sounds** (and **ventilator/chest tube** checks when relevant) before routine tasks when respiratory risk is rising.
- Use the respiratory topic hub (**`respiratory`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.
- Link review: [ABG basics](LESSON:abg-interpretation-basics-hy) · [COPD oxygen](LESSON:copd-exacerbation-oxygen) · [pneumonia oxygenation](LESSON:pneumonia-oxygenation) · [pleural effusion & chest tubes](LESSON:pleural-effusion-chest-tubes) · [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons)

**Takeaway drill:** Name the **life threat**, the **two objective findings** you recheck first, and the **single escalation** that matches policy—then pick the answer that matches that sequence.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Oxygen Delivery Devices** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

NCLEX tests **FiO₂ ranges** (approximate), **humidification** needs, **masks vs cannula**, **Venturi** for controlled oxygen in **CO₂ retainers** when the stem specifies, and **when to escalate** to reservoir or high-flow pathways—avoid **100% NRB** for every stable client.

Eliminate answers that **skip airway assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show instability, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Nasal cannula (NC)**: comfort for mild hypoxemia; estimate FiO₂ only when the item uses textbook ranges—follow **ordered flow** and **SpO₂ targets**.
- **Simple mask**: higher FiO₂ than NC but variable with minute ventilation; watch skin breakdown and anxiety.
- **Venturi mask**: **precise FiO₂** when ordered—useful when titration must be controlled (retainer scenarios when specified).
- **Non-rebreather with reservoir**: acute significant hypoxemia themes—verify **tight seal**, **bag inflation**, and **RT/provider** coordination.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.
- **Oxygen toxicity / hyperoxia in retainers**: somnolence with **rising PaCO₂**—titrate to **ordered SpO₂ targets** and escalate ventilation support when acidosis worsens.
- **Tension pneumothorax**: hypotension, **distended neck veins**, unilateral absent sounds, tracheal shift (late) → emergency decompression themes; do not delay for routine imaging when stem portrays classic shock.
- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.
- **Ventilator alarms**: **never silence without assessment**; treat **client** before **machine** unless circuit disconnect is obvious and safe to fix immediately.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **device use** (NC, mask, CPAP), **signs of worsening** (worsening dyspnea, new cyanosis, fever), **infection prevention** at home when applicable, and **when to call emergency services**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Oxygen Therapy Safety and Titration** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Expect **ordered SpO₂ targets**, **reassessment after titration**, **ABG** when ventilation failure is implied, and **BiPAP** escalation themes—not reflexive **max SpO₂** in **chronic CO₂ retention** vignettes.

Eliminate answers that **skip airway assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show instability, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Titrate to order**: document device, flow, FiO₂ mode, and response (RR, work of breathing, mentation).
- **Fire safety**: oxygen in use signage and education where applicable; no smoking or open flames near oxygen.
- **Humidification**: dry mucosa and thick secretions—follow protocol for high flows or prolonged therapy.
- **Skin/nose integrity**: rotate NC prongs, pad mask straps, assess pressure areas.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.
- **Oxygen toxicity / hyperoxia in retainers**: somnolence with **rising PaCO₂**—titrate to **ordered SpO₂ targets** and escalate ventilation support when acidosis worsens.
- **Tension pneumothorax**: hypotension, **distended neck veins**, unilateral absent sounds, tracheal shift (late) → emergency decompression themes; do not delay for routine imaging when stem portrays classic shock.
- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.
- **Ventilator alarms**: **never silence without assessment**; treat **client** before **machine** unless circuit disconnect is obvious and safe to fix immediately.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **device use** (NC, mask, CPAP), **signs of worsening** (worsening dyspnea, new cyanosis, fever), **infection prevention** at home when applicable, and **when to call emergency services**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Hypoxia vs Hypoxemia
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Hypoxia vs Hypoxemia | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Clinical review: Hypoxia vs Hypoxemia — Respiratory. Board-style clinical judgment: priorities, red flags, and first-line nursing actions.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Hypoxia vs Hypoxemia** (Respiratory) links assessment, oxygen delivery, airway management, infection prevention, and critical care monitoring to nursing judgment: protect **airway and breathing**, titrate **oxygen per orders and targets**, recognize **acute deterioration** (silent chest, fatigue, rising CO₂, tension pneumothorax), support **ventilation and chest drainage** safely, and escalate when respiratory failure threatens perfusion or mentation.

Canadian items may use **metric** units and provincial isolation wording; prioritization logic matches NCLEX-RN.

**Pathway context (RN, Canada).** This lesson supports NCLEX-RN preparation with Canada-friendly practice framing (SI measures where shown, interprofessional norms). Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **inspection, work of breathing, SpO₂, ABG when shown, breath sounds, ventilator/chest tube cues, and mentation** to identify respiratory emergencies and complications.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, RT and provider plans, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **complete obstruction**, **tension pneumothorax**, **massive hemothorax**, **ventilator failure**, **sudden desaturation with altered LOC**, or **rapid clinical decline**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Hypoxia vs Hypoxemia** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Boards separate **low PaO₂/SpO₂** from **impaired oxygen delivery** (Hb, CO, perfusion). Choose answers that **treat the mechanism**—transfusion themes, **100% O₂ for CO** when specified, **perfusion** support in shock—not only “raise the cannula flow” when saturation is misleading.

Eliminate answers that **skip airway assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show instability, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Hypoxemia**: low PaO₂ (and often low SpO₂ when pulse ox is reliable).
- **Hypoxia**: tissues starved of O₂—can occur with **normal SpO₂** if **Hb is low**, **CO** shifts curve, or **perfusion** is poor.
- **Nursing link**: pair **SpO₂** with **BP, lactate, urine output, mental status**, and **Hb** when the stem provides them.
- **Safety**: never ignore **altered mentation** or **rising lactate** because SpO₂ “looks fine.”

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.
- **Oxygen toxicity / hyperoxia in retainers**: somnolence with **rising PaCO₂**—titrate to **ordered SpO₂ targets** and escalate ventilation support when acidosis worsens.
- **Tension pneumothorax**: hypotension, **distended neck veins**, unilateral absent sounds, tracheal shift (late) → emergency decompression themes; do not delay for routine imaging when stem portrays classic shock.
- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.
- **Ventilator alarms**: **never silence without assessment**; treat **client** before **machine** unless circuit disconnect is obvious and safe to fix immediately.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **device use** (NC, mask, CPAP), **signs of worsening** (worsening dyspnea, new cyanosis, fever), **infection prevention** at home when applicable, and **when to call emergency services**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client scenario related to **normal PaO₂ but cellular hypoxia in severe anemia or CO poisoning vignettes** brings respiratory risks to the foreground. You pair **focused assessment** (work of breathing, auscultation, SpO₂ trend, mental status, device and tube checks) with **vitals and labs** when provided, maintain **oxygen and isolation per orders**, follow **ventilation and drainage plans**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** You compare competing tasks. The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually **assess airway**, **support breathing**, **verify tube/circuit**, **relieve life threats**, or **obtain critical diagnostics**—rather than routine comfort measures alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **stridor with drooling**, **sudden unilateral absent breath sounds with shock**, **high-pressure vent alarm with desaturation**, or **massive hemothorax**, prioritize **life-saving sequences** that match unit protocol and NCLEX-RN expectations.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **RR + work of breathing + SpO₂ + mentation + breath sounds** (and **ventilator/chest tube** checks when relevant) before routine tasks when respiratory risk is rising.
- Use the respiratory topic hub (**`respiratory`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Early vs Late Signs of Respiratory Distress** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Reward recognizing **fatigue** and **impending arrest**: paradoxical breathing, **silent chest** in asthma, **bradypnea** after tachypnea, sudden **normalization** of work of breathing with worsening mentation. Eliminate answers that **skip airway assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show instability, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Early**: anxiety, tachypnea, accessory muscles, mild desaturation, tripod positioning.
- **Late**: altered LOC, bradypnea, **cyanosis**, **absent breath sounds**, hypotension, signs of **fatigue**.
- **Pediatric/older adult**: subtle fatigue—watch trends and feeding tolerance when relevant.
- **Escalate** when late signs appear: notify provider, prepare emergency support, continuous monitoring.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.
- **Oxygen toxicity / hyperoxia in retainers**: somnolence with **rising PaCO₂**—titrate to **ordered SpO₂ targets** and escalate ventilation support when acidosis worsens.
- **Tension pneumothorax**: hypotension, **distended neck veins**, unilateral absent sounds, tracheal shift (late) → emergency decompression themes; do not delay for routine imaging when stem portrays classic shock.
- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.
- **Ventilator alarms**: **never silence without assessment**; treat **client** before **machine** unless circuit disconnect is obvious and safe to fix immediately.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **device use** (NC, mask, CPAP), **signs of worsening** (worsening dyspnea, new cyanosis, fever), **infection prevention** at home when applicable, and **when to call emergency services**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Airway Obstruction and Stridor
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Airway Obstruction and Stridor | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Clinical review: Airway Obstruction and Stridor — Respiratory. Board-style clinical judgment: priorities, red flags, and first-line nursing actions.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Airway Obstruction and Stridor** (Respiratory) links assessment, oxygen delivery, airway management, infection prevention, and critical care monitoring to nursing judgment: protect **airway and breathing**, titrate **oxygen per orders and targets**, recognize **acute deterioration** (silent chest, fatigue, rising CO₂, tension pneumothorax), support **ventilation and chest drainage** safely, and escalate when respiratory failure threatens perfusion or mentation.

Canadian items may use **metric** units and provincial isolation wording; prioritization logic matches NCLEX-RN.

**Pathway context (RN, Canada).** This lesson supports NCLEX-RN preparation with Canada-friendly practice framing (SI measures where shown, interprofessional norms). Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **inspection, work of breathing, SpO₂, ABG when shown, breath sounds, ventilator/chest tube cues, and mentation** to identify respiratory emergencies and complications.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, RT and provider plans, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **complete obstruction**, **tension pneumothorax**, **massive hemothorax**, **ventilator failure**, **sudden desaturation with altered LOC**, or **rapid clinical decline**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Airway Obstruction and Stridor** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Tests **do not agitate**, **do not lay flat** when epiglottitis is suggested, **immediate team activation**, and **advanced airway** readiness. Differentiate **upper airway** obstruction from **lower airway** wheeze patterns.

Eliminate answers that **skip airway assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show instability, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Stridor** = upper airway narrowing until proven otherwise—think infection, edema, foreign body, anaphylaxis per stem.
- **Position**: allow **position of comfort**; avoid forced supine when obstruction is suspected.
- **Interventions**: high-alert monitoring, **epinephrine** themes for anaphylaxis, **racemic epi** or **heliox** only per orders when shown.
- **Never**: blind finger sweeps in awake partial obstruction; follow BLS/ALS scope.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.
- **Oxygen toxicity / hyperoxia in retainers**: somnolence with **rising PaCO₂**—titrate to **ordered SpO₂ targets** and escalate ventilation support when acidosis worsens.
- **Tension pneumothorax**: hypotension, **distended neck veins**, unilateral absent sounds, tracheal shift (late) → emergency decompression themes; do not delay for routine imaging when stem portrays classic shock.
- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.
- **Ventilator alarms**: **never silence without assessment**; treat **client** before **machine** unless circuit disconnect is obvious and safe to fix immediately.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **device use** (NC, mask, CPAP), **signs of worsening** (worsening dyspnea, new cyanosis, fever), **infection prevention** at home when applicable, and **when to call emergency services**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client scenario related to **inspiratory stridor and drooling in a febrile client sitting forward** brings respiratory risks to the foreground. You pair **focused assessment** (work of breathing, auscultation, SpO₂ trend, mental status, device and tube checks) with **vitals and labs** when provided, maintain **oxygen and isolation per orders**, follow **ventilation and drainage plans**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** You compare competing tasks. The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually **assess airway**, **support breathing**, **verify tube/circuit**, **relieve life threats**, or **obtain critical diagnostics**—rather than routine comfort measures alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **stridor with drooling**, **sudden unilateral absent breath sounds with shock**, **high-pressure vent alarm with desaturation**, or **massive hemothorax**, prioritize **life-saving sequences** that match unit protocol and NCLEX-RN expectations.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **RR + work of breathing + SpO₂ + mentation + breath sounds** (and **ventilator/chest tube** checks when relevant) before routine tasks when respiratory risk is rising.
- Use the respiratory topic hub (**`respiratory`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.

sections.body:
Examiners use **Incentive Spirometry and Pulmonary Hygiene** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Choose **IS frequency**, **pain control** so deep breathing is possible, **early mobility**, and **coughing/splinting** teaching. IS alone without **pain management** or **mobility** is a common wrong answer.

Eliminate answers that **skip airway assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show instability, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Teach goal**: sustained inspiratory effort per device instructions; document response.
- **Bundle**: turn, cough, deep breathe, ambulate per orders; incentive spirometry supports the bundle—it is not a substitute for walking.
- **Contraindications/caution**: follow stem—clients unable to cooperate, certain esophageal surgeries, or severe baseline obstruction may need modified plans per orders.
- **Red flags**: fever, productive cough, unilateral decreased sounds—think pneumonia or atelectasis beyond hygiene alone.

- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.
- **Oxygen toxicity / hyperoxia in retainers**: somnolence with **rising PaCO₂**—titrate to **ordered SpO₂ targets** and escalate ventilation support when acidosis worsens.
- **Tension pneumothorax**: hypotension, **distended neck veins**, unilateral absent sounds, tracheal shift (late) → emergency decompression themes; do not delay for routine imaging when stem portrays classic shock.
- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.
- **Ventilator alarms**: **never silence without assessment**; treat **client** before **machine** unless circuit disconnect is obvious and safe to fix immediately.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **device use** (NC, mask, CPAP), **signs of worsening** (worsening dyspnea, new cyanosis, fever), **infection prevention** at home when applicable, and **when to call emergency services**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

- **Teaching**: splinting, deep breathing, cough hygiene, hydration when not contraindicated.

- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.

- **Tension pneumothorax**: hypotension, **distended neck veins**, unilateral absent sounds, tracheal shift (late) → emergency decompression themes; do not delay for routine imaging when stem portrays classic shock.
- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.
- **Ventilator alarms**: **never silence without assessment**; treat **client** before **machine** unless circuit disconnect is obvious and safe to fix immediately.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **device use** (NC, mask, CPAP), **signs of worsening** (worsening dyspnea, new cyanosis, fever), **infection prevention** at home when applicable, and **when to call emergency services**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

Examiners use **Acute Bronchitis vs Chronic Bronchitis** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Acute bronchitis: **supportive care**, **avoid unnecessary antibiotics** unless bacterial complication is suggested. Chronic bronchitis (COPD phenotype): **exacerbation bundles**, **oxygen titration**, **smoking cessation**—do not merge the two management paths.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Upper Respiratory Infections** (Respiratory) links assessment, oxygen delivery, airway management, infection prevention, and critical care monitoring to nursing judgment: protect **airway and breathing**, titrate **oxygen per orders and targets**, recognize **acute deterioration** (silent chest, fatigue, rising CO₂, tension pneumothorax), support **ventilation and chest drainage** safely, and escalate when respiratory failure threatens perfusion or mentation.

Canadian items may use **metric** units and provincial isolation wording; prioritization logic matches NCLEX-RN.

**Pathway context (RN, Canada).** This lesson supports NCLEX-RN preparation with Canada-friendly practice framing (SI measures where shown, interprofessional norms). Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **inspection, work of breathing, SpO₂, ABG when shown, breath sounds, ventilator/chest tube cues, and mentation** to identify respiratory emergencies and complications.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, RT and provider plans, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **complete obstruction**, **tension pneumothorax**, **massive hemothorax**, **ventilator failure**, **sudden desaturation with altered LOC**, or **rapid clinical decline**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Upper Respiratory Infections** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Tests **symptomatic care**, **red flag education** (respiratory distress, dehydration, stiff neck), **droplet/contact** basics when applicable, and **antibiotic stewardship**—viral URI does not need routine antibiotics.

Eliminate answers that **skip airway assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show instability, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Comfort**: saline nasal care, hydration, antipyretics per order and age-safe dosing.
- **Teaching**: hand hygiene, return precautions for breathing difficulty, ear pain, or prolonged fever.
- **Vulnerable populations**: infants, elderly, immunocompromised—lower threshold to escalate assessment.
- **Work/school**: return per local public health guidance when the stem references outbreaks.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.
- **Oxygen toxicity / hyperoxia in retainers**: somnolence with **rising PaCO₂**—titrate to **ordered SpO₂ targets** and escalate ventilation support when acidosis worsens.
- **Tension pneumothorax**: hypotension, **distended neck veins**, unilateral absent sounds, tracheal shift (late) → emergency decompression themes; do not delay for routine imaging when stem portrays classic shock.
- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.
- **Ventilator alarms**: **never silence without assessment**; treat **client** before **machine** unless circuit disconnect is obvious and safe to fix immediately.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **device use** (NC, mask, CPAP), **signs of worsening** (worsening dyspnea, new cyanosis, fever), **infection prevention** at home when applicable, and **when to call emergency services**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client scenario related to **school-age child with rhinorrhea and fever; parent asks for antibiotics** brings respiratory risks to the foreground. You pair **focused assessment** (work of breathing, auscultation, SpO₂ trend, mental status, device and tube checks) with **vitals and labs** when provided, maintain **oxygen and isolation per orders**, follow **ventilation and drainage plans**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** You compare competing tasks. The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually **assess airway**, **support breathing**, **verify tube/circuit**, **relieve life threats**, or **obtain critical diagnostics**—rather than routine comfort measures alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **stridor with drooling**, **sudden unilateral absent breath sounds with shock**, **high-pressure vent alarm with desaturation**, or **massive hemothorax**, prioritize **life-saving sequences** that match unit protocol and NCLEX-RN expectations.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **RR + work of breathing + SpO₂ + mentation + breath sounds** (and **ventilator/chest tube** checks when relevant) before routine tasks when respiratory risk is rising.
- Use the respiratory topic hub (**`respiratory`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.
- Link review: [ABG basics](LESSON:abg-interpretation-basics-hy) · [COPD oxygen](LESSON:copd-exacerbation-oxygen) · [pneumonia oxygenation](LESSON:pneumonia-oxygenation) · [pleural effusion & chest tubes](LESSON:pleural-effusion-chest-tubes) · [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons)

**Takeaway drill:** Name the **life threat**, the **two objective findings** you recheck first, and the **single escalation** that matches policy—then pick the answer that matches that sequence.

Examiners use **Laryngitis and Epiglottitis** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Pneumothorax (Simple, Tension) Recognition and Management** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

**Tension pneumothorax** requires **decompression** and **team activation**—not “wait for CXR” when classic instability is portrayed. **Simple** pneumothorax may be observation vs chest tube per orders—match severity to intervention.

Eliminate answers that **skip airway assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show instability, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Tension**: hypotension, **distended neck veins**, **tracheal deviation** (late), absent breath sounds, **tachycardia**—emergency needle decompression themes then tube per scope.
- **Simple**: may be small and stable—monitoring, oxygen, repeat imaging per orders.
- **Nursing**: high FiO₂ when ordered, prepare chest tube tray, continuous monitoring, frequent reassessment.
- **After intervention**: check for **air leak**, **re-expansion** symptoms, and bleeding at insertion site.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.
- **Oxygen toxicity / hyperoxia in retainers**: somnolence with **rising PaCO₂**—titrate to **ordered SpO₂ targets** and escalate ventilation support when acidosis worsens.
- **Tension pneumothorax**: hypotension, **distended neck veins**, unilateral absent sounds, tracheal shift (late) → emergency decompression themes; do not delay for routine imaging when stem portrays classic shock.
- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.
- **Ventilator alarms**: **never silence without assessment**; treat **client** before **machine** unless circuit disconnect is obvious and safe to fix immediately.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **device use** (NC, mask, CPAP), **signs of worsening** (worsening dyspnea, new cyanosis, fever), **infection prevention** at home when applicable, and **when to call emergency services**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client scenario related to **trauma client with absent unilateral breath sounds, hypotension, and distended neck veins** brings respiratory risks to the foreground. You pair **focused assessment** (work of breathing, auscultation, SpO₂ trend, mental status, device and tube checks) with **vitals and labs** when provided, maintain **oxygen and isolation per orders**, follow **ventilation and drainage plans**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** You compare competing tasks. The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually **assess airway**, **support breathing**, **verify tube/circuit**, **relieve life threats**, or **obtain critical diagnostics**—rather than routine comfort measures alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **stridor with drooling**, **sudden unilateral absent breath sounds with shock**, **high-pressure vent alarm with desaturation**, or **massive hemothorax**, prioritize **life-saving sequences** that match unit protocol and NCLEX-RN expectations.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **RR + work of breathing + SpO₂ + mentation + breath sounds** (and **ventilator/chest tube** checks when relevant) before routine tasks when respiratory risk is rising.
- Use the respiratory topic hub (**`respiratory`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Hemothorax Nursing Care
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Hemothorax Nursing Care | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Clinical review: Hemothorax Nursing Care — Respiratory. Board-style clinical judgment: priorities, red flags, and first-line nursing actions.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Hemothorax Nursing Care** (Respiratory) links assessment, oxygen delivery, airway management, infection prevention, and critical care monitoring to nursing judgment: protect **airway and breathing**, titrate **oxygen per orders and targets**, recognize **acute deterioration** (silent chest, fatigue, rising CO₂, tension pneumothorax), support **ventilation and chest drainage** safely, and escalate when respiratory failure threatens perfusion or mentation.

Canadian items may use **metric** units and provincial isolation wording; prioritization logic matches NCLEX-RN.

**Pathway context (RN, Canada).** This lesson supports NCLEX-RN preparation with Canada-friendly practice framing (SI measures where shown, interprofessional norms). Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **inspection, work of breathing, SpO₂, ABG when shown, breath sounds, ventilator/chest tube cues, and mentation** to identify respiratory emergencies and complications.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, RT and provider plans, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **complete obstruction**, **tension pneumothorax**, **massive hemothorax**, **ventilator failure**, **sudden desaturation with altered LOC**, or **rapid clinical decline**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Hemothorax Nursing Care** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Pair **massive hemothorax** with **volume resuscitation**, **type and screen**, **chest tube** to **evacuate blood**, and **surgical** escalation when drainage exceeds thresholds in the stem. Monitor for **hypovolemic shock**.

Eliminate answers that **skip airway assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show instability, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Assessment**: decreased breath sounds, dull percussion, hypoxia, signs of shock when blood loss is significant.
- **Chest tube**: monitor **hourly drainage**, **clots**, and **autotransfusion** only if protocol/orders allow.
- **Labs**: serial Hgb/Hct, type and cross, lactate trends.
- **Teaching**: injury prevention for stable contexts; return precautions for recurrent bleeding or dyspnea.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.
- **Oxygen toxicity / hyperoxia in retainers**: somnolence with **rising PaCO₂**—titrate to **ordered SpO₂ targets** and escalate ventilation support when acidosis worsens.
- **Tension pneumothorax**: hypotension, **distended neck veins**, unilateral absent sounds, tracheal shift (late) → emergency decompression themes; do not delay for routine imaging when stem portrays classic shock.
- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.
- **Ventilator alarms**: **never silence without assessment**; treat **client** before **machine** unless circuit disconnect is obvious and safe to fix immediately.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **device use** (NC, mask, CPAP), **signs of worsening** (worsening dyspnea, new cyanosis, fever), **infection prevention** at home when applicable, and **when to call emergency services**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Chest Tube Troubleshooting** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Differentiate **system leak** vs **patient leak** (bubbles with cough vs at rest). **Dislodgement** requires **occlusive dressing** and **immediate notification**. **Lost swing** may mean **kinks**, **clamps left on**, or **disconnection**—never clamp arbitrarily without orders except emergency teaching items.

Eliminate answers that **skip airway assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show instability, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Air leak**: document timing with respiration; maintain **water seal** unless waterless system; notify if worsening.
- **Drainage**: sudden cessation may mean **clot** or **tube malposition**—assess tubing, client position, and vitals.
- **Dislodgement**: sterile petroleum gauze **three sides taped** classic teaching; emergency support.
- **Milking/stripping**: only per modern facility policy—many items prefer **no aggressive stripping**.

- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.
- **Oxygen toxicity / hyperoxia in retainers**: somnolence with **rising PaCO₂**—titrate to **ordered SpO₂ targets** and escalate ventilation support when acidosis worsens.
- **Tension pneumothorax**: hypotension, **distended neck veins**, unilateral absent sounds, tracheal shift (late) → emergency decompression themes; do not delay for routine imaging when stem portrays classic shock.
- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.
- **Ventilator alarms**: **never silence without assessment**; treat **client** before **machine** unless circuit disconnect is obvious and safe to fix immediately.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Mechanical Ventilation Basics** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Know **high-pressure** vs **low-pressure** alarm causes at a nursing level: **secretions**, **kink**, **biting**, **bronchospasm**, **pneumothorax**, **right mainstem intubation** vs **leak** in cuff or circuit. First actions are **assessment + manual breath per RT scope + notify**—not blind changes to settings outside order.

Eliminate answers that **skip airway assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show instability, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **ABCDE**: airway patency, tube position cues, circuit integrity, bilateral breath sounds, SpO₂/ETCO₂ if available.
- **Sedation/analgesia**: RASS targets per orders; avoid oversedation that masks neurologic decline.
- **Positioning**: HOB elevation unless contraindicated; oral care and VAP bundle themes.
- **Communication**: explain sensations when awake; use call bell or writing board when unable to speak.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.
- **Oxygen toxicity / hyperoxia in retainers**: somnolence with **rising PaCO₂**—titrate to **ordered SpO₂ targets** and escalate ventilation support when acidosis worsens.
- **Tension pneumothorax**: hypotension, **distended neck veins**, unilateral absent sounds, tracheal shift (late) → emergency decompression themes; do not delay for routine imaging when stem portrays classic shock.
- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.
- **Ventilator alarms**: **never silence without assessment**; treat **client** before **machine** unless circuit disconnect is obvious and safe to fix immediately.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **device use** (NC, mask, CPAP), **signs of worsening** (worsening dyspnea, new cyanosis, fever), **infection prevention** at home when applicable, and **when to call emergency services**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Assessment**: RSBI themes when provided, mental status, ABG trends, hemodynamics.
- **Collaboration**: RT-driven protocols; nurse monitors during **SBT** and stops per criteria.
- **Post-extubation**: stridor management themes, voice assessment, swallow screen before PO when policy requires.
- **Nutrition/energy**: avoid large gastric loads before weaning when aspiration risk is highlighted.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.
- **Oxygen toxicity / hyperoxia in retainers**: somnolence with **rising PaCO₂**—titrate to **ordered SpO₂ targets** and escalate ventilation support when acidosis worsens.
- **Tension pneumothorax**: hypotension, **distended neck veins**, unilateral absent sounds, tracheal shift (late) → emergency decompression themes; do not delay for routine imaging when stem portrays classic shock.
- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.
- **Ventilator alarms**: **never silence without assessment**; treat **client** before **machine** unless circuit disconnect is obvious and safe to fix immediately.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **device use** (NC, mask, CPAP), **signs of worsening** (worsening dyspnea, new cyanosis, fever), **infection prevention** at home when applicable, and **when to call emergency services**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Tracheostomy Care and Emergency Management
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Tracheostomy Care and Emergency | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Clinical review: Tracheostomy Care and Emergency — Respiratory. Board-style clinical judgment: priorities, red flags, and first-line nursing actions.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Tracheostomy Care and Emergency Management** (Respiratory) links assessment, oxygen delivery, airway management, infection prevention, and critical care monitoring to nursing judgment: protect **airway and breathing**, titrate **oxygen per orders and targets**, recognize **acute deterioration** (silent chest, fatigue, rising CO₂, tension pneumothorax), support **ventilation and chest drainage** safely, and escalate when respiratory failure threatens perfusion or mentation.

Canadian items may use **metric** units and provincial isolation wording; prioritization logic matches NCLEX-RN.

**Pathway context (RN, Canada).** This lesson supports NCLEX-RN preparation with Canada-friendly practice framing (SI measures where shown, interprofessional norms). Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **inspection, work of breathing, SpO₂, ABG when shown, breath sounds, ventilator/chest tube cues, and mentation** to identify respiratory emergencies and complications.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, RT and provider plans, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **complete obstruction**, **tension pneumothorax**, **massive hemothorax**, **ventilator failure**, **sudden desaturation with altered LOC**, or **rapid clinical decline**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Tracheostomy Care and Emergency Management** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

**Fresh trach** emergency differs from **mature stoma**: may need **oral intubation** if **reinsertion fails**—follow facility algorithm. **Obstruction**: **pass suction catheter** to test patency; **inner cannula** cleaning when applicable.

Eliminate answers that **skip airway assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show instability, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept

- **Speaking valves**: only when cuff deflated per order and RT evaluation—never guess.

- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.

- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.
- **Ventilator alarms**: **never silence without assessment**; treat **client** before **machine** unless circuit disconnect is obvious and safe to fix immediately.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**

- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

Examiners use **Respiratory Acidosis vs Alkalosis (Clinical Correlation)** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Acidosis + high PaCO₂**: improve **minute ventilation** (reversible causes first when ordered: naloxone, BiPAP, treat bronchospasm).
- **Alkalosis + low PaCO₂**: treat underlying cause; **paper bag rebreathing** is outdated—items favor **anxiolytics** or **oxygen** when appropriate to etiology.
- **Chronic retainers**: interpret **baseline** when provided; avoid overcorrection narratives that ignore ventilation.
- **Safety**: repeat ABG after major changes when stem implies monitoring expectations.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.

- **Tension pneumothorax**: hypotension, **distended neck veins**, unilateral absent sounds, tracheal shift (late) → emergency decompression themes; do not delay for routine imaging when stem portrays classic shock.
- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

- **VTE prophylaxis**: important—follow orders in immobilized clients.

- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.

You pair **focused assessment** (work of breathing, auscultation, SpO₂ trend, mental status, device and tube checks) with **vitals and labs** when provided, maintain **oxygen and isolation per orders**, follow **ventilation and drainage plans**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: NGN Respiratory Case Studies
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: NGN Respiratory Case Studies | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Clinical review: NGN Respiratory Case Studies — Respiratory. Board-style clinical judgment: priorities, red flags, and first-line nursing actions.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**NGN Respiratory Case Studies** (Respiratory) links assessment, oxygen delivery, airway management, infection prevention, and critical care monitoring to nursing judgment: protect **airway and breathing**, titrate **oxygen per orders and targets**, recognize **acute deterioration** (silent chest, fatigue, rising CO₂, tension pneumothorax), support **ventilation and chest drainage** safely, and escalate when respiratory failure threatens perfusion or mentation.

Canadian items may use **metric** units and provincial isolation wording; prioritization logic matches NCLEX-RN.

**Pathway context (RN, Canada).** This lesson supports NCLEX-RN preparation with Canada-friendly practice framing (SI measures where shown, interprofessional norms). Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **inspection, work of breathing, SpO₂, ABG when shown, breath sounds, ventilator/chest tube cues, and mentation** to identify respiratory emergencies and complications.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, RT and provider plans, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **complete obstruction**, **tension pneumothorax**, **massive hemothorax**, **ventilator failure**, **sudden desaturation with altered LOC**, or **rapid clinical decline**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **NGN Respiratory Case Studies** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

NGN rewards **data synthesis**: connect **SpO₂ + ABG + lung sounds + mentation + vitals** before choosing **interventions**, **notifications**, or **education**. Use **least invasive effective** steps first unless the stem shows **arrest** risk.

Eliminate answers that **skip airway assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show instability, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Highlight**: trend lines (RR, SpO₂, BP), new orders, and abnormal labs together.
- **Cognitive stack**: stabilize → diagnose support → teach—in that order when instability exists.
- **Cues**: **rising lactate**, **widening A-a gradient**, **unilateral absent sound** change the differential.
- **Reflect**: document focused assessment and response to each intervention when item asks for “next documentation.”

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.
- **Oxygen toxicity / hyperoxia in retainers**: somnolence with **rising PaCO₂**—titrate to **ordered SpO₂ targets** and escalate ventilation support when acidosis worsens.
- **Tension pneumothorax**: hypotension, **distended neck veins**, unilateral absent sounds, tracheal shift (late) → emergency decompression themes; do not delay for routine imaging when stem portrays classic shock.
- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.
- **Ventilator alarms**: **never silence without assessment**; treat **client** before **machine** unless circuit disconnect is obvious and safe to fix immediately.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **device use** (NC, mask, CPAP), **signs of worsening** (worsening dyspnea, new cyanosis, fever), **infection prevention** at home when applicable, and **when to call emergency services**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.body:
Examiners use **Respiratory Assessment for Nurses (Inspection, Palpation, Percussion, Auscultation)** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Inspection**: RR pattern, nasal flaring, retractions, speaking in full sentences vs one-word answers, skin colour, anxiety level.
- **Palpation**: chest expansion symmetry; **tracheal deviation** with tension themes; **subcutaneous emphysema** after trauma or line placement.
- **Percussion**: **dullness** suggests fluid or consolidation; **hyperresonance** suggests air trapping or pneumothorax when paired with exam findings.
- **Auscultation**: compare sides; note **absent sounds**, **crackles**, **wheezes**, **pleural rub**; correlate with SpO₂ and work of breathing.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.
- **Oxygen toxicity / hyperoxia in retainers**: somnolence with **rising PaCO₂**—titrate to **ordered SpO₂ targets** and escalate ventilation support when acidosis worsens.
- **Tension pneumothorax**: hypotension, **distended neck veins**, unilateral absent sounds, tracheal shift (late) → emergency decompression themes; do not delay for routine imaging when stem portrays classic shock.
- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.
- **Ventilator alarms**: **never silence without assessment**; treat **client** before **machine** unless circuit disconnect is obvious and safe to fix immediately.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **device use** (NC, mask, CPAP), **signs of worsening** (worsening dyspnea, new cyanosis, fever), **infection prevention** at home when applicable, and **when to call emergency services**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Oxygen Delivery Devices** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

NCLEX tests **FiO₂ ranges** (approximate), **humidification** needs, **masks vs cannula**, **Venturi** for controlled oxygen in **CO₂ retainers** when the stem specifies, and **when to escalate** to reservoir or high-flow pathways—avoid **100% NRB** for every stable client.

Eliminate answers that **skip airway assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show instability, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Nasal cannula (NC)**: comfort for mild hypoxemia; estimate FiO₂ only when the item uses textbook ranges—follow **ordered flow** and **SpO₂ targets**.
- **Simple mask**: higher FiO₂ than NC but variable with minute ventilation; watch skin breakdown and anxiety.
- **Venturi mask**: **precise FiO₂** when ordered—useful when titration must be controlled (retainer scenarios when specified).
- **Non-rebreather with reservoir**: acute significant hypoxemia themes—verify **tight seal**, **bag inflation**, and **RT/provider** coordination.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.
- **Oxygen toxicity / hyperoxia in retainers**: somnolence with **rising PaCO₂**—titrate to **ordered SpO₂ targets** and escalate ventilation support when acidosis worsens.
- **Tension pneumothorax**: hypotension, **distended neck veins**, unilateral absent sounds, tracheal shift (late) → emergency decompression themes; do not delay for routine imaging when stem portrays classic shock.
- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.
- **Ventilator alarms**: **never silence without assessment**; treat **client** before **machine** unless circuit disconnect is obvious and safe to fix immediately.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **device use** (NC, mask, CPAP), **signs of worsening** (worsening dyspnea, new cyanosis, fever), **infection prevention** at home when applicable, and **when to call emergency services**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Exam relevance

Eliminate answers that **skip airway assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show instability, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Titrate to order**: document device, flow, FiO₂ mode, and response (RR, work of breathing, mentation).
- **Fire safety**: oxygen in use signage and education where applicable; no smoking or open flames near oxygen.
- **Humidification**: dry mucosa and thick secretions—follow protocol for high flows or prolonged therapy.
- **Skin/nose integrity**: rotate NC prongs, pad mask straps, assess pressure areas.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.
- **Oxygen toxicity / hyperoxia in retainers**: somnolence with **rising PaCO₂**—titrate to **ordered SpO₂ targets** and escalate ventilation support when acidosis worsens.
- **Tension pneumothorax**: hypotension, **distended neck veins**, unilateral absent sounds, tracheal shift (late) → emergency decompression themes; do not delay for routine imaging when stem portrays classic shock.
- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.
- **Ventilator alarms**: **never silence without assessment**; treat **client** before **machine** unless circuit disconnect is obvious and safe to fix immediately.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **device use** (NC, mask, CPAP), **signs of worsening** (worsening dyspnea, new cyanosis, fever), **infection prevention** at home when applicable, and **when to call emergency services**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

us-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Hypoxia vs Hypoxemia | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Clinical review: Hypoxia vs Hypoxemia — Respiratory. Board-style clinical judgment: priorities, red flags, and first-line nursing actions.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Hypoxia vs Hypoxemia** (Respiratory) links assessment, oxygen delivery, airway management, infection prevention, and critical care monitoring to nursing judgment: protect **airway and breathing**, titrate **oxygen per orders and targets**, recognize **acute deterioration** (silent chest, fatigue, rising CO₂, tension pneumothorax), support **ventilation and chest drainage** safely, and escalate when respiratory failure threatens perfusion or mentation.

US NCLEX-RN items often test **unstable vs stable**, **airway first**, and **ventilator/chest tube** emergencies.

**Pathway context (RN, United States).** Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **inspection, work of breathing, SpO₂, ABG when shown, breath sounds, ventilator/chest tube cues, and mentation** to identify respiratory emergencies and complications.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, RT and provider plans, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **complete obstruction**, **tension pneumothorax**, **massive hemothorax**, **ventilator failure**, **sudden desaturation with altered LOC**, or **rapid clinical decline**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Hypoxia vs Hypoxemia** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Boards separate **low PaO₂/SpO₂** from **impaired oxygen delivery** (Hb, CO, perfusion). Choose answers that **treat the mechanism**—transfusion themes, **100% O₂ for CO** when specified, **perfusion** support in shock—not only “raise the cannula flow” when saturation is misleading.

Eliminate answers that **skip airway assessment**, **delay escalation** when data show instability, or **delegate** RN-level clinical judgment inappropriately. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Hypoxemia**: low PaO₂ (and often low SpO₂ when pulse ox is reliable).
- **Hypoxia**: tissues starved of O₂—can occur with **normal SpO₂** if **Hb is low**, **CO** shifts curve, or **perfusion** is poor.
- **Nursing link**: pair **SpO₂** with **BP, lactate, urine output, mental status**, and **Hb** when the stem provides them.
- **Safety**: never ignore **altered mentation** or **rising lactate** because SpO₂ “looks fine.”

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Airway compromise**: stridor, inability to handle secretions, **silent chest**, sudden voice change → activate emergency support per protocol; **position of comfort** when upper obstruction suspected.
- **Oxygen toxicity / hyperoxia in retainers**: somnolence with **rising PaCO₂**—titrate to **ordered SpO₂ targets** and escalate ventilation support when acidosis worsens.
- **Tension pneumothorax**: hypotension, **distended neck veins**, unilateral absent sounds, tracheal shift (late) → emergency decompression themes; do not delay for routine imaging when stem portrays classic shock.
- **Chest tube**: sudden **increased** air leak, **1500 mL**-type rapid blood loss thresholds when stem states policy, **dislodgement** → occlusive dressing and immediate notification.
- **Ventilator alarms**: **never silence without assessment**; treat **client** before **machine** unless circuit disconnect is obvious and safe to fix immediately.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **device use** (NC, mask, CPAP), **signs of worsening** (worsening dyspnea, new cyanosis, fever), **infection prevention** at home when applicable, and **when to call emergency services**.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when airway, breathing, or perfusion is threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client scenario related to **normal PaO₂ but cellular hypoxia in severe anemia or CO poisoning vignettes** brings respiratory risks to the foreground. You pair **focused assessment** (work of breathing, auscultation, SpO₂ trend, mental status, device and tube checks) with **vitals and labs** when provided, maintain **oxygen and isolation per orders**, follow **ventilation and drainage plans**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.
